title: Module 4: Affirmations - Recognizing Strengths Without Cheerleading
learning_objective: Learn to offer genuine affirmations that recognize patient strengths, efforts, and values without cheerleading, premature reassurance, or empty praise
scenario_context: Affirmations recognize and amplify patient strengths, efforts, and positive qualities. Genuine affirmations are specific, sincere, and build self-efficacy. They differ from cheerleading (overly enthusiastic), false reassurance ('I'm sure you can do it'), or flattery (generic praise). Good affirmations notice what's already there.

persona_config.name: Um,
persona_config.background: Affirmations recognize and amplify patient strengths, efforts, and positive qualities. Genuine affirmations are specific, sincere, and build self-efficacy. They differ from cheerleading (overly enthusiastic), false reassurance ('I'm sure you can do it'), or flattery (generic praise). Good affirmations notice what's already there.

node_1.persona_text: I've been thinking a lot about what we talked about last time. I actually looked up some information about quitting smoking. I haven't made any decisions, but I wanted to understand my options better.

choice_points.option_text: That's great! I'm so proud of you! This is a really big step!

feedback.immediate: This is cheerleading - overly enthusiastic and focused on your feelings ('I'm proud') rather than their action. It can feel patronizing and creates pressure.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: You took the initiative to educate yourself about quitting, even without being sure you're ready. That takes thoughtfulness and effort.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This affirmation is specific (what they did), genuine (acknowledges uncertainty), and recognizes the qualities it demonstrates (initiative, thoughtfulness, effort).
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: Looking up information is a good first step.

feedback.immediate: This acknowledges the action but doesn't affirm the strengths or qualities it demonstrates. It's MI-adherent but a fuller affirmation would be more powerful.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_2a.persona_text: Um, thanks. I mean, it's not that big of a deal. I just googled some stuff.

choice_points.option_text: Well, I think it IS a big deal! Every journey starts with a first step!

feedback.immediate: Continuing to cheerlead after they've minimized it can make them more uncomfortable. Match their pace - offer specific, understated recognition instead.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: You took time to look into it, even though you're still weighing your options. That says something about how seriously you're considering this.

feedback.immediate: Good recovery! This is more understated and specific, which may match their comfort level better. It notices their action without making it bigger than they're ready for.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_2b.persona_text: I guess I did. I wasn't sure if it would help, but I figured knowledge can't hurt, right? I learned about nicotine replacement therapy and some of the withdrawal symptoms to expect.

choice_points.option_text: So you approached this methodically - gathering information before making decisions. And you were willing to face potentially uncomfortable information about withdrawal.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This affirms the qualities demonstrated - being methodical and brave enough to face difficult information. This builds self-efficacy.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: That's wonderful! Knowledge is power!

feedback.immediate: This is cheerleading paired with a cliché. It's generic rather than specific to what they did. Genuine affirmations are specific and sincere.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: You learned about NRT and withdrawal symptoms.

feedback.immediate: This is a reflection, not an affirmation. It's MI-adherent and appropriate, but you missed an opportunity to recognize the strength they demonstrated. Both reflections and affirmations have their place.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

node_2c.persona_text: Yeah. I wanted to know what I'd be getting into. Withdrawal sounds pretty rough, honestly. But at least now I know.

choice_points.option_text: You kept researching even when you found information that was intimidating. That takes courage.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This affirms a specific quality (courage) in the face of a specific challenge (intimidating information). This kind of affirmation builds confidence.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: Don't worry! Lots of people get through withdrawal just fine! You'll be okay!

feedback.immediate: This is false reassurance - minimizing their concerns and making promises you can't keep. It dismisses their realistic concerns rather than affirming their courage in facing them.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: The information about withdrawal concerned you.

feedback.immediate: This is an accurate reflection. While MI-adherent, you missed the opportunity to affirm their persistence in researching despite finding concerning information.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

node_3a.persona_text: I am taking it seriously. More seriously than I expected to, honestly. Especially since my granddaughter was born last week. She's... perfect.

choice_points.option_text: Congratulations! Now you have the perfect reason to quit!

feedback.immediate: While congratulations is appropriate, saying they 'have the perfect reason to quit' prescribes their motivation and creates pressure. Let them tell you what it means to them.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: Congratulations on becoming a grandparent. It sounds like meeting her has stirred something in you.

feedback.immediate: Good! This offers appropriate congratulations while reflecting their experience without prescribing what it should mean. Both MI-adherent options work - the next one adds an affirmation.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: Congratulations. You're noticing that becoming a grandparent is shifting your perspective on this. You're paying attention to those shifts.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This acknowledges the milestone, reflects their experience, and affirms their self-awareness - noticing their own shifts is a strength worth recognizing.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_3b.persona_text: I hadn't thought of it that way, but yeah, I guess I do like to understand things before I jump in. And the withdrawal stuff was hard to read, but I needed to know what I'd be facing.

choice_points.option_text: You don't shy away from difficult realities. You'd rather face them head-on, even when they're uncomfortable.

feedback.immediate: Perfect! This affirms a character strength (facing reality) that will serve them well in making and implementing a difficult decision. This builds self-efficacy.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: See? You're already showing the kind of strength and determination you'll need to quit!

feedback.immediate: This jumps ahead to assuming they'll quit and frames the affirmation in terms of future success. Stay present-focused - affirm what they're demonstrating now.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_3c.persona_text: I guess. I'm not sure 'power' is the word I'd use. I feel kind of overwhelmed, actually.

choice_points.option_text: You're feeling overwhelmed by the information, which makes sense given how serious this decision is. And you're sitting with it anyway rather than avoiding it.

feedback.immediate: Good recovery! This validates their feeling while gently affirming that they're engaging despite being overwhelmed. This is more attuned to where they are.
feedback.learning_note: Validation acknowledges the patient's experience without judgment.

choice_points.option_text: Don't be overwhelmed! You can do this! I believe in you!

feedback.immediate: This dismisses their feeling and continues cheerleading. When someone says they're overwhelmed, validate that experience rather than trying to cheer them out of it.
feedback.learning_note: Validation acknowledges the patient's experience without judgment.

node_3d.persona_text: Right. I wanted to be realistic about it. My friend quit last year and said the first two weeks were hell. I needed to know if I could handle that.

choice_points.option_text: You're not just gathering information - you're honestly assessing whether you're up for this challenge. That's wise.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This affirms both their self-assessment process and the wisdom of that approach. It recognizes a strength they're demonstrating.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: Of course you can handle it! You're stronger than you think!

feedback.immediate: This is false reassurance - claiming to know they can handle something they're uncertain about. It dismisses their legitimate question about their capabilities.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_3e.persona_text: Courage? I don't know about that. But I figured if I'm going to consider this, I should know the worst-case scenario, not just the success stories.

choice_points.option_text: You wanted the full picture - the difficult parts alongside the hopeful parts. That's a realistic, mature approach.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This affirms their realistic approach without overselling it as courage (which they rejected). It meets them where they are.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: That's exactly the right attitude! You're going to succeed because you're being so realistic!

feedback.immediate: This is cheerleading and makes premature predictions about success. Affirm what they're doing now without jumping to future outcomes.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_3f.persona_text: I hope you're right, but I don't know. My friend who quit said it was the hardest thing he'd ever done. I'm not sure I'm as strong as he is.

choice_points.option_text: You're comparing yourself to your friend and wondering if you have what it takes. What strengths have helped you get through other difficult times?

feedback.immediate: Good recovery! Instead of more reassurance, you're inviting them to identify their own strengths and past successes. This builds genuine confidence.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: You are absolutely strong enough! Stop doubting yourself!

feedback.immediate: Continuing to insist they're capable when they're expressing doubt creates pressure and disconnection. Help them discover their own strengths instead.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_3g.persona_text: It did. But I kept reading anyway. If I'm going to make a decision, I want it to be informed, not based on fantasy.

choice_points.option_text: You value making informed decisions based on reality, even when reality is daunting. That's a strength that will serve you well, whatever you decide.

feedback.immediate: Perfect! This affirms both a value (informed decisions) and how it manifests as strength (facing daunting reality). The phrase 'whatever you decide' honors their autonomy.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: Smart! Being informed means you'll be more likely to succeed!

feedback.immediate: While acknowledging their approach, this makes assumptions about outcomes and sounds cheerleader-ish. Stay focused on the strength itself, not predicted results.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_4a.persona_text: I mean... maybe. But I don't want to quit just because everyone thinks I should. I need to want it for myself, or it won't stick.

choice_points.option_text: You understand something important - that change works best when the motivation comes from inside you, not from external pressure. That self-awareness is valuable.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This affirms their insight about autonomous motivation without pressuring them. You're recognizing their wisdom about the change process.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: That's fine! As long as you quit eventually, it doesn't matter what motivates you!

feedback.immediate: This dismisses their important insight and reveals goal-focused thinking (wanting them to quit) over process-focused (supporting their autonomy). This breaks MI spirit.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_4b.persona_text: It has. When I hold her, I think about being around to see her grow up. And I think about what kind of grandfather I want to be.

choice_points.option_text: Becoming a grandfather is making you think about the kind of person and role model you want to be. Those reflections matter.

feedback.immediate: Good! This reflects their thinking and gently affirms that this reflection is meaningful. Both options are MI-adherent - the next one is a fuller affirmation.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: You're connecting this decision to your values about being a grandfather. That kind of values-based thinking often leads to lasting change.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This affirms their values-based thinking process and notes (without guaranteeing) that this approach often works. It's informational rather than prescriptive.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_4c.persona_text: I am. It's kind of surprising to me, actually. I didn't expect her birth to affect me this much. But something shifted when I held her.

choice_points.option_text: You're open to being affected and changed by this experience, even though it surprised you. That openness is a strength.

feedback.immediate: Perfect! This affirms their openness to being transformed by experience - a quality that serves change well. You're noticing and naming a strength.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: That's beautiful! Grandchildren change everything! Now you'll definitely want to quit!

feedback.immediate: This is cheerleading and prescribes what they should feel/do. Let them discover what this means for them rather than telling them.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_4d.persona_text: I guess I do try to face things rather than avoid them. It doesn't always feel good, but it's how I operate.

choice_points.option_text: That approach has probably served you well in other areas of your life.

feedback.immediate: Good! This helps them see this strength as something that's been useful beyond just this situation. This builds confidence in their capabilities.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: Exactly! And that's why you're going to be successful at quitting!

feedback.immediate: This jumps to conclusions about what they'll do and whether they'll succeed. Keep affirmations present-focused on the strengths they're demonstrating now.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_4e.persona_text: Hold on. I haven't decided to quit yet. I'm just... looking into it.

choice_points.option_text: You're right - I got ahead of you there. You're in the information-gathering phase, not the decision-making phase yet.

feedback.immediate: Good recovery! Acknowledging when you got ahead of the patient and correcting course maintains trust and honors their autonomy.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: Okay, okay, but you're clearly thinking about it seriously, which is basically the first step!

feedback.immediate: This continues to push them forward when they just pulled back. Respect their pace - affirm where they actually are, not where you want them to be.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_4f.persona_text: Yeah. I'm trying to sit with it even though it's uncomfortable. I think that's important.

choice_points.option_text: You're willing to sit with discomfort because you know it's part of working through a big decision. That takes maturity.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This affirms their ability to tolerate discomfort in service of thoughtful decision-making - an important capacity for change.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_4g.persona_text: Thanks. I've been burned before by jumping into things without thinking them through. I'm trying to be different about this.

choice_points.option_text: You're applying what you've learned from past experiences - using that wisdom to approach this differently. That's growth.

feedback.immediate: Perfect! This affirms their ability to learn from experience and apply that learning - a key strength for successful change.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: Good! Learning from mistakes is how we grow!

feedback.immediate: While true, this is generic and cliché rather than specific to what they shared. Specific affirmations are more powerful and meaningful.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_4h.persona_text: But can you know that? I mean, you don't know me that well. What if I'm not strong enough?

choice_points.option_text: You're right - I can't know that. What I do notice is that you're asking yourself honest questions about your capabilities rather than assuming you can or can't do this. That kind of honest self-assessment is actually a strength.

feedback.immediate: Excellent recovery! You acknowledge the false reassurance was inappropriate, then offer a genuine affirmation based on what you actually observe - their honest self-assessment.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: I just have a feeling about you! You're going to do great!

feedback.immediate: Continuing with feelings and predictions when they've questioned your basis is not helpful. Affirm real, observable strengths instead.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_4i.persona_text: Thanks. I've always believed in facing reality, even when it's hard. My dad taught me that.

choice_points.option_text: That's a valuable inheritance from your father - the courage to face reality. And now you're applying that same principle to this decision.

feedback.immediate: Beautiful! This affirms the strength while honoring its source (father) and noting how they're applying it now. This connects the affirmation to their deeper values.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_4j.persona_text: I don't think being realistic guarantees success. That seems like magical thinking.

choice_points.option_text: You're right - there are no guarantees. What I notice is that you're approaching this thoughtfully and realistically, which are qualities that serve people well when they're facing difficult decisions.

feedback.immediate: Good recovery! You acknowledge their point and then offer a more genuine affirmation - noting qualities without promising outcomes.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_4k.persona_text: Hmm. I guess... I got through my divorce. That was hell. And I raised my daughter mostly on my own after that. I've handled hard things before.

choice_points.option_text: So you've already demonstrated that you can get through really difficult periods - a divorce and single parenting. Those weren't easy, and you came through them.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This affirms their past resilience specifically and concretely. Helping patients recognize their own track record builds genuine confidence.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: See? You're definitely strong enough to quit smoking!

feedback.immediate: This jumps too quickly to conclusions about smoking. Let them make connections at their own pace - affirm the strengths without prescribing how to apply them.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_4l.persona_text: Thanks. I appreciate that you're not pushing me one way or the other. Just helping me think it through.

choice_points.option_text: That's important to you - having space to make your own decision without pressure. I respect that.

feedback.immediate: Perfect! You reflect their value (autonomy) and explicitly honor it. This strengthens the therapeutic relationship and demonstrates MI spirit.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_4m.persona_text: Maybe. I'm not really thinking about success or failure right now. I'm just trying to figure out what I want.

choice_points.option_text: You're focusing on the right question first - what do you actually want - rather than worrying about whether you can achieve it. That's a wise approach.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This affirms their process - focusing on values/desires before strategies/outcomes. This is sophisticated thinking worth recognizing.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_5a.persona_text: I've learned that the hard way. When I've made changes because people nagged me, they never lasted. I need to own it.

choice_points.option_text: You've learned through experience what makes change last for you - it has to come from inside. That self-knowledge is powerful.

feedback.immediate: Perfect! This affirms their experiential learning and self-knowledge - both strengths that predict successful change when they're ready.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_5b.persona_text: Actually, it does matter to me. A lot. I'm not interested in quitting just to check a box.

choice_points.option_text: You're right, and I apologize for missing that. The quality of how you make this decision matters as much as the decision itself. That integrity is important to you.

feedback.immediate: Good recovery! Apologizing and then affirming their value for integrity shows respect and repairs the relationship.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_5c.persona_text: They do. I want to be someone she can be proud of. Someone who's around and healthy.

choice_points.option_text: You're letting your love for her and your vision of the grandfather you want to be inform how you think about this. That's grounding this in what matters most.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This affirms that they're using their deepest values to guide their thinking - the kind of motivation that sustains difficult change.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_5d.persona_text: I guess it is. I'm usually pretty set in my ways, but this... she opened something up in me I didn't know was there.

choice_points.option_text: You're discovering capacities in yourself you didn't know you had. That discovery is meaningful.

feedback.immediate: Beautiful! This affirms their openness to self-discovery and transformation - a profound strength for someone 'usually set in their ways.'
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_5e.persona_text: Please don't tell me what I'll definitely want. I need to figure that out for myself.

choice_points.option_text: You're absolutely right, and I apologize. Your ability to advocate for your autonomy, even with me, shows real clarity about what you need.

feedback.immediate: Excellent recovery! Apologizing and then affirming their self-advocacy shows respect and models that it's okay to push back when someone oversteps.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_5f.persona_text: It has. When I got divorced, people told me to ignore the problems and hope they went away. But I knew I needed to face reality, even though it hurt.

choice_points.option_text: You trusted your own judgment over others' advice, even when your path was more painful. That takes both courage and wisdom.

feedback.immediate: Perfect! This affirms multiple related strengths - trusting themselves, choosing the harder right path, courage, and wisdom.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_5g.persona_text: I haven't decided to quit. Can you stop assuming I'm going to?

choice_points.option_text: I apologize - I keep getting ahead of where you actually are. You're exploring, not deciding. I'll do better at staying with you where you are.

feedback.immediate: Good recovery! Acknowledging the pattern, apologizing sincerely, and committing to change shows respect for their process and autonomy.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_5h.persona_text: Right. I'm gathering information. Making an informed decision matters to me, whether that decision is to quit or not.

choice_points.option_text: You value making thoughtful, informed decisions regardless of the outcome. That integrity in your decision-making process is admirable.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This affirms their value for process integrity and explicitly honors that the outcome is theirs to determine.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_5i.persona_text: I think it is. My whole life I've made impulsive decisions and regretted them. I'm trying to do this differently.

choice_points.option_text: You're breaking a lifelong pattern - that's significant. Instead of impulsivity, you're choosing reflection and patience.

feedback.immediate: Perfect! This affirms the difficulty and significance of changing long-standing patterns. This recognizes real growth.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_5j.persona_text: Yeah. I'm tired of repeating the same mistakes. I want this to be different.

choice_points.option_text: That desire to break patterns and do things differently - that's coming from you, from your own learning. That's the foundation for real change.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This affirms that their motivation is intrinsic and based on their own learning - the strongest foundation for sustained change.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_5k.persona_text: Sure, but I'm trying to do more than just grow. I'm trying to make a really difficult decision carefully.

choice_points.option_text: You're right - I was being too general. What you're doing specifically is taking time to carefully consider a major life decision, drawing on your past experiences to approach it more wisely. That's more than just 'growth' - that's intentional transformation.

feedback.immediate: Excellent recovery! You acknowledge the generic affirmation was insufficient and offer a much more specific one that captures what they're actually doing.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_5l.persona_text: Okay, that makes sense. I guess I am trying to be honest with myself about what I can and can't do.

choice_points.option_text: That honest self-assessment, even when the answers are uncertain, is a real strength. It's how people make decisions they can live with.

feedback.immediate: Perfect! This affirms the specific skill and its value for decision-making, without making predictions about outcomes.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_5m.persona_text: He'd be proud of me for thinking this through carefully, I think. He always said 'face the facts, then decide.'

choice_points.option_text: You're honoring your father by applying his wisdom to your own life. And you're doing exactly what he taught - facing the facts first.

feedback.immediate: Beautiful! This affirms how they're living out their father's values while making their own decision. This connects the affirmation to something deeply meaningful.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_5n.persona_text: Yeah, that's more realistic. Thanks for not over-promising. That actually makes me trust you more.

choice_points.option_text: That tells me something important about you - you value authenticity over flattery. That keeps you grounded in reality.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! You've turned their feedback into an affirmation - recognizing that valuing authenticity is itself a strength.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_5o.persona_text: I did. They were awful times, but I survived them. Maybe... maybe that means I could handle quitting too. I don't know.

choice_points.option_text: You're starting to wonder if the resilience that got you through those other challenges might be available to you here too. That's worth considering.

feedback.immediate: Perfect! This reflects their emerging thought without pushing it. You're affirming that this consideration is worth having, not that they should definitely quit.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_5p.persona_text: That's not what I said. I just said I've handled hard things. Smoking is different.

choice_points.option_text: You're right - I jumped ahead. Each challenge is different. What you did identify is that you have a track record of getting through difficult periods. Whether or how that applies here is for you to figure out.

feedback.immediate: Good recovery! You acknowledge overstepping while still affirming what they actually said - that they have resilience - without prescribing its application.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_end_good.persona_text: This has been helpful. I feel like you see the strengths I'm trying to use here, and that makes me more confident in my process, whatever I end up deciding.

node_end_moderate.persona_text: Okay. I appreciate you hearing me when I pushed back. That helps.

node_end_uncomfortable.persona_text: Look, I appreciate your enthusiasm, but this doesn't feel helpful anymore. I think I need to process this on my own.

node_end_resistance.persona_text: You're not listening to me. I'm leaving.
